coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots synthesized through a hydrothermal method with different isomers
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Po-Chih
Ting, Yu-Xuan
Gu, Siyong
Ashraf Gandomi, Yasser
Hsieh, Chien-Te
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have developed a novel fabrication technique for preparing N-functionalized carbon nanodots (CNDs) through one-pot hydrothermal route using various isomers, exhibiting highly intense fluorescence under blue light illumination. Three types of isomers, o-, m-, and p-phenylenediamine (PD), serving as the carbon and nitrogen sources were employed where the N/C atomic ratio in the CNDs structure was precisely tuned within the range of 20.2–25.7 at.% via tailoring the isomer precursors. The application of the m-PD precursor resulted in implanting the “lattice N” functionalities while the o-PD precursor enabled the formation of N-oxide groups. The CND suspensions in highly-polarity solvents (e.g., water and ethanol) emit intense fluorescence with ultrahigh quantum yield (QY: 85‒99%), whereas the fluorescence from the CND suspensions in propylene glycol methyl ether acetate is significantly quenched. The ultrahigh QY is majorly due to an intense radiative emission mechanism induced by the substitutional N atoms (via high electron-withdrawing) and N- and O-rich edge groups as the major contributors for boosting the affinity to high-polar solvents. Given the widespread applications of quantum dots as high-performance nanomaterials, this study paves the way for engineering N-functionalized CNDs to be used in optical, sensing, energy storage/conversion, and biological devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen functionalization
Carbon nanodots
Photoluminescence emission
Hydrothermal synthesis
Isomer precursor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL emission of N-functionalized CND suspensions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of N-functionalized CNDs through the hydrothermal route.
HR-TEM micrographs of (a) o-CND, (b) m-CND, and (c) p-CND samples.
FT-IR spectra of o-CND, m-CND, and p-CND samples.
XPS N 1 s spectra of (a) o-CND, (b) m-CND, and (c) p-CND samples, decomposed by a multiple Gaussian function.
Possible growth mechanism of CND samples from different isomers through the hydrothermal route, based on various phenazine-like ladder structures.
UV–vis absorbance spectra of o-CND, m-CND, and p-CND suspensions in (a) water, (b) ethanol, (c) PGMEA, and (d) acetone.
PL emission spectra of o-CND, m-CND, and p-CND suspensions in (a) water, (b) ethanol, (c) PGMEA, and (d) acetone under an excitation of 450 nm.
The QY values of o-CND, m-CND, and p-CND suspensions in (a) water, (b) ethanol, (c) PGMEA, and (d) acetone under an excitation of 450 nm. The inset shows the photograph of o-CND suspension at 450 nm.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots synthesized through a hydrothermal method with different isomers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Taoyuan 32003, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bFujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Functional Materials and Applications, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xiamen University of Technology, Xiamen 361024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA 02142, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Chemical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA 02142, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have developed a novel fabrication technique for preparing N-functionalized carbon nanodots (CNDs) through one-pot hydrothermal route using various isomers, exhibiting highly intense fluorescence under blue light illumination. Three types of isomers, o-, m-, and p-phenylenediamine (PD), serving as the carbon and nitrogen sources were employed where the N/C atomic ratio in the CNDs structure was precisely tuned within the range of 20.2–25.7 at.% via tailoring the isomer precursors. The application of the m-PD precursor resulted in implanting the “lattice N” functionalities while the o-PD precursor enabled the formation of N-oxide groups. The CND suspensions in highly-polarity solvents (e.g., water and ethanol) emit intense fluorescence with ultrahigh quantum yield (QY: 85‒99%), whereas the fluorescence from the CND suspensions in propylene glycol methyl ether acetate is significantly quenched. The ultrahigh QY is majorly due to an intense radiative emission mechanism induced by the substitutional N atoms (via high electron-withdrawing) and N- and O-rich edge groups as the major contributors for boosting the affinity to high-polar solvents. Given the widespread applications of quantum dots as high-performance nanomaterials, this study paves the way for engineering N-functionalized CNDs to be used in optical, sensing, energy storage/conversion, and biological devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots including semiconductor quantum dots [1–4] and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/carbon nanodots (CNDs) [5–17], are promising nanomaterials for biological labeling, bio-imaging, fluorescent probes, energy storage/conversion devices, and optoelectronic apparatuses. By converting two-dimensional graphene sheets into zero-dimensional (0D) CNDs and bottom-up growing CNDs through either thermal pyrolysis or a gas-phase deposition technique, CNDs are synthesized with superior 0D nanomaterial properties inheriting the unique attributes of the graphene material [18–20]. Also, CNDs are capable of exhibiting remarkable semiconductor characteristics and strong luminescence emissions under certain conditions; therefore, they are commonly considered “carbon nano-lights” [21]. Compared to metallic quantum dots, CNDs are relatively non-toxic and are environmentally friendly [22]. In addition, the CNDs exhibit good biocompatibility, facile modification capability, stable chemical inertness, and high solubility (in aqueous solutions) [22].

For many applications, it is critical to functionalize CNDs and enhance their quantum yields (QYs). Recently, doping carbon lattices with heteroatoms such as electron-rich N atoms has been accomplished through in-plane substitution of N atoms, making graphene an n-type semiconductor [23–25]. Furthermore, other advanced techniques have been developed for synthesizing N-doped GQDs/CNDs, including hydrothermal route using NH4OH as the source of nitrogen [23], chemical vapor deposition with pyridine as the source of both C and N [24], thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and urea [25] or ethanolamine precursors [26], and thermal pyrolysis of core-shell nanoparticles followed by dialysis [27]. It has been shown that N-doping or amino functionalization significantly alters the optical properties and fluorescence intensity of CNDs. To the best of our knowledge, there are very limited prior works dedicated for N-functionalized CNDs via one-pot hydrothermal route. In particular, the influence of carbon isomer precursors on the CNDs fluorescent performance under the blue-light illumination still requires an in-depth investigation.

In this work, we have developed an efficient hydrothermal method to carbonize three types of isomers (i.e., o-, m-, and p-phenylenediamine (o-PD, m-PD, and p-PD)) in order to form N-functionalized CNDs. Herein o-PD, m-PD, and p-PD served as the carbon and nitrogen sources during the synthesis procedure. We have chosen this combination of isomers since pioneering studies have demonstrated the efficacy of these precursors for synthesizing carbon nanodots using hydrothermal [28,29], solvothermal [30], microwave-assisted [31], and electrochemical bottom-up synthesis techniques [32]. However, the CNDs prepared in this work possess high amidation level as compared to the CND samples reported in the literature (i.e., N/C ratio: 5‒15 at.% [23]). We have demonstrated that the selection of isomer precursor alters the amidation level along with the N-containing functionalities formed at the edges as well as the N-dopants onto the basal plane or into C=C sp2-network. Accordingly, this work aims at exploring the influence of isomer precursor on the surface amidation level and establishing tunable photoluminescence (PL) emission of CNDs in different solvents under the blue-light illumination. To illustrate the tunable PL, we have determined the key parameters for tailoring PL emissions and QYs from the N-functionalized CND suspensions.

To synthesize the N-functionalized CNDs, first, three kinds of precursors, o-PD, m-PD, and p-PD (purity: 99%) were mixed with deionized water and then stirred using a magnetic bar at 150 rpm. Second, the homogeneous suspensions were placed into a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (75 mL) in an oven. The hydrothermal synthesis process was performed at 300 °C for 2 h; followingly, the solutions were naturally cooled down to the ambient temperature. The as-prepared CNDs were cooled down and filtered through a porous separator (nominal pore size: 20 nm) to remove any residual impurities. In order to eliminate the remaining precursor and any side products, the solutions were further dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off value of 100‒500 Da for 24 h. Followingly, the as-purified CNDs were dispersed in ultrapure water before conducting any experiment. Finally, the GQD suspensions were freeze-dried at −30 °C for 72 h. Throughout this paper, the as-prepared CNDs samples have been labeled as “o-CND”, “m-CND”, and “p-CND”, based on the corresponding precursor (i.e., o-PD, m-PD, and p-PD, respectively). A schematic diagram illustrating the hydrothermal synthesis procedure of CNDs (i.e., o-CND sample) has been provided in Fig. 1 . The synthesis route for other sample configurations has also been shown in the Electronic Supporting Information (see Fig. S1).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Talos F200s) was employed to characterize the N-functionalized CND samples with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fison VG ESCA210) equipped with Mg-Kα radiation emitter, was used to analyze the chemical composition of CND samples. Herein the C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s spectra were deconvoluted through a multiple Gaussian function. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired on Nicolet 380 spectrometer. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using an Agilent Cary 60 spectrometer with wavelength scan rate of 60 nm/min. Each CND suspension was prepared by dispersing the CND powder (50 mg) in different solvents (500 mL) including deionized water, ethanol (85 vol.%), acetone, and propylene glycol methyl ether acetate (PGMEA). The properties of different solvents used in this study are tabulated in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL emission of N-functionalized CND suspensions
ce:section.ce:para:
The CND suspensions were vibrated in different solvents through a high-performance ultrasonic bathing at ambient temperature for 30 min. The PL emission spectra of CND suspensions were obtained employing a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000 FLS920P) under 450 nm excitation. The QY of CND suspensions was measured with respect to the Coumarin (C9H6O2, molecular weight: 146; QY: 73% at 373 nm excitation). Subsequently, the QY value of CND suspension was determined using the following equation [33]:
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y r
×
[
(
P L
a r e a / O D
)
s
/
(
P L
a r e a / O D
)
r
]
×
ϕ s 2
/
ϕ r 2

In Eq. (1), “s” and “r” subscripts denote the CND sample and the reference (i.e., Coumarin), respectively. Herein ϕ represents the reflective index of solvent, “PL area” reflects the PL emission spectral area, and “OD” resembles the absorbance value.

HR-TEM studies were carried out for the CND samples (i.e., o-CND, m-CND, and p-CND samples), as illustrated in Fig. 2 (a)‒2(c). According to Fig. 2, the CNDs prepared through the one-pot hydrothermal synthesis method demonstrate good uniformity. Although all the CND samples possess a quasi-spherical shape, the particle size distribution among these samples varied significantly. The average diameter of the quasi-spherical particles demonstrated the following order: p-CND (47.2 nm) > m-CND (35.2 nm) > o-CND (2.8 nm). To characterize the particle size distributions of as-prepared CNDs, the dynamic laser scattering technique was employed, as illustrated in Fig. S2. The mean particle size measured was in good agreement with that observed from HR-TEM. Since the CND powders used for preparing various suspensions remained unchanged during the synthesis procedure, it is clear that the morphology of as-prepared CNDs is affected by the isomer type. Indeed, the growth rate of CNDs strongly depends on the isomer type during the hydrothermal process and is altered by the positioning of the amino groups within the aromatic ring. The crystallinity of CND samples was characterized by the selected area diffraction (SAD), as shown in inset of Fig. 2(a)‒2(c). The SAD patterns including only circular rings (see Fig. 2) reveals that all CNDs are of polycrystalline domain within the selected area [34].

The FT-IR spectra (see Fig. 3 ) were obtained for exploring the distribution of the surface functional groups on the CND samples. The broad band ranging from 2800 to 3600 cm−1 belongs to the characteristic vibration of N‒H, C‒H and O‒H bonds [35]. Also, the strong peaks at 1602 and 1535 cm−1 are usually assigned to C=N and C=C stretching vibrations carbon structure, respectively [36]. Finally, the single transmittance peak within the range of 1150–1300 cm−1 corresponds to C‒O stretch of –COOH and confirms the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the CND samples [37]. Based on the analysis of FT-IR spectra, all samples are composed of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities, attached to the edge or basal plane of CNDs.

XPS measurements were employed to uncover the chemical compositions and distribution of surface functional groups induced by different isomers. The XPS survey spectra of all CND samples (see Fig. S3) shows predominant C 1 s peak at ca. 284 eV, N 1 s peak at ca. 400 eV, and O 1 s peak at ca. 532 eV [35,36]. The O/C atomic ratio had the following sequence: o-CND (53.6 at.%) > m-CND (19.0 at.%) > p-CND (17.5 at.%), whereas the N/C ratio demonstrated an opposite trend (i.e., p-CND (25.7 at.%) > m-CND (22.3 at.%) > o-CND (20.2 at.%)). This observation reveals that the location of ‒NH2 groups within the isomers plays a crucial role influencing the oxidation/amidation level on the functionalized CNDs. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra (see Fig. S4) confirm the presence of C‒C/C=C (ca. 284.6 eV), C‒N (ca. 285.8 eV), C‒OH (ca. 286.6 eV), and C=O (ca. 287.2 eV), referring to different bonding types to C atoms [38]. Accordingly, it can be concluded that o-CND sample contains a large amount of oxygen functionalities, exhibiting higher hydrophilicity as compared to other samples. According to Fig. S5, the O 1 s spectra are comprised of two major peaks at ca. 531.8 and ca. 532.5 eV, mainly originated from the presence of C=O and C–O bonds, respectively [39].

The N 1 s spectra, as shown in Fig. 4 , were deconvoluted into three signals, assigning to predominant pyrrolic or pyridinic N (ca. 399.6 eV), quaternary N (ca. 400.4 eV), and N-oxides (e.g., O=C‒N) at ca. 401.5 eV [38,40,41]. Among all the samples, the o-CND sample consists of pyrrolic/pyridinic N (40%), quaternary N (36%), and N-oxides (24%); however, m-CND sample is mainly dominated by the pyrrolic/pyridinic N (61%) and quaternary N (33%). Considering the structure of “chemical N”, the pyrrolic/pyridinic N can be ascribed to the formation of aromatic C=N–C where the N atom tends to bond with two carbon atoms or pyridine moiety, while the quaternary N has higher tendency to form tertiary N‒(C)3 where the N atom is bonded to three sp2 carbon atoms (i.e., graphitic N) [42]. Based on this observation, three important points can be pointed out: (i) the hydrothermal synthesis technique not only inserts “lattice N” into the basal planes but also decorates “chemical N” on the edges of CNDs, (ii) employing the m-PD precursor, the “lattice N” functionalities can be implanted within the structure, and (iii) utilizing o-PD precursor enables the implantation of N-oxide groups.

To describe the details N-functionalization of CNDs, one possible growth mechanism is proposed (see Fig. 5 ). According to XPS analysis, the o-CND sample possesses highest oxidation extent (O/C ratio: 53.6 at.%) among all the samples. Indeed, the o-CND sample consisted of a large number of amino groups with very high tendency to react with the carboxylic acid groups forming intermediate amide or amine-like species (‘‘chemical N’’) through the nucleophilic substitution [43,44]. For the p-CND as well as the m-CND samples, the hydrothermal carbonization is advantageous for the formation of in-situ doping sites such as pyrrolic and pyridinic N promoting the in-plane N substitution (“lattice N”).

It is well established that the phenylenediamines contain two primary amino groups that can be used in the oxidative linkage of monomers [45]. In the presence of water, the polymerization of the monomers may lead to various oxygen-containing constitutional units (in particular, the quinonediimines or benzoquinones [46]). Especially at oxygen-rich environment, both amino groups may be involved in producing a phenazine-like ladder structure [47,48], as shown in Fig. 5. The ladder could be visualized as two entwined polyaniline chains. This mechanism has also been confirmed through the mathematical models developed based on phenylenediamine isomer [49,50]. The o-CNDs can thus be formed via breaking the N–H and C–H bonds of o-PD precursor and re-forming the N=C/N–C bonds through engaging O–H, C–O, C=O, O=C–O bonds between o-PDs in a sequential manner (see Fig. 5) throughout the entire hydrothermal process. It is critical to note that during the hydrothermal heating, the oxygen atoms (from solvent and oxygen-rich environment) will also participate in the synthetic reaction of o-CNDs [31]. Therefore, the synthesis of o-CND samples follows the process of micromolecule polymerizing into large molecules polymer containing plentiful C elements and abundant unsaturated bonds [51].

Considering the polymerization process of o-CND based on phenazine morphology, the shape of the ladder phenazine structure is believed to affect the formation of CNDs during the hydrothermal process. Thus, the phenylenediamine isomers combine to form the mono-bonds. Subsequently, the mono-bonds and the amino groups undergo an oxidative process to form the phenazine structure. Followingly, the 2D structure of the N-doped CNDs is formed with small lateral size and well-defined edge groups [32]. Accordingly, the distribution of N functionalities can be finely tuned through adjusting the carbon- and nitrogen-containing isomer precursors.

Analyzing the UV–Vis absorption spectra (the wavelength ranging from 200 to 450 nm) recorded for the as-prepared CND suspensions reveal that three distinct absorption bands can be identified in different solvents, as shown in Fig. 6 . The absorption bands are mainly divided into three regions: (i) 200‒250 nm, (ii) 300‒350 nm, and (iii) 400‒450 nm, which mainly originated from π→π* transition of C=C of sp2 domain in sp3 C matrix, n→π* transition of C=O or C=N, and an absorption edge induced by n→π* transition of non-bonding electrons of adatoms, respectively [52–54]. Indeed, the absorption band can be considered as a multiple band gap configuration of functionalized CNDs, i.e., multiple chromophoric band-gap structure, originated from crystalline size, oxygenated/nitrogenated defect-related emissive traps, as well as the grain boundaries [8]. It is well established that the band gap of aromatic molecules commonly exhibit a clear reduction in magnitude decreasing from 7 eV (single benzene ring) to 2 eV (a cluster of 20 aromatic rings) [55]. As observed from the UV–Vis spectra, both particle size and surface heterogeneity (e.g., O-/N-functionalities and solvent type) influences the optical band gap structure of CNDs, and subsequently alters the optical and electrical properties [8].

Fig. 7 demonstrates the PL spectra of different CND suspensions in water, ethanol, acetone, and PGMEA excited at 450 nm. The o-CND suspension in water displays a quasi-symmetric peak ranging from 500 to 650 nm and a slight tailing from 650 to 700 nm. It is important to note that the o-CND suspension emit higher fluorescence as compared to other samples. This observation also holds for the CND suspensions in the ethanol. The inset of Fig. 7(a) and 7(b) shows that o-CND suspensions are capable of emitting bright and intense yellow color, whereas the other two suspensions merely illuminate weak blue-green fluorescence under the blue-light illumination. When the CND suspensions were dispersed in acetone, the PL intensities were dramatically decreased exhibiting the following sequence: p-CND > m-CND > o-CND (see Fig. 7(c)). Considering the CND suspensions in PGMEA, it is clear that the PL is quenched for all the suspensions, as depicted in Fig. 7(d). Therefore, it can be concluded that the solvent type strongly affects the PL emission from the N-functionalized CND suspensions.

The PL spectra of different CND suspensions emit a multiple emission within a wide wavelength range, referring to the multiple chromophoric band-gap structure in the CND suspensions [56,57]. The multiple band-gap structure is strongly dependent on the photon rejection from the highest occupied molecular orbital to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital gap. The decoration of O and doping of N atoms tend to generate additional “defect sites”, enabling additional energy levels and effectively creating new electron transition pathways in the inter-band structure of CNDs [58]. Under the blue-light illumination, the absorption of photons by the localized π electron in double bonds (mainly C=C) results in an electron‒hole pair (exciton) structure upon electron transition. This excitation empowers the light emission through radiative recombination as a result of vibrational relaxation. The excited electrons usually undergo inter-band transition from a higher conduction band to a lower conduction band emitting visible light by radiative recombination [38]. Indeed, such an inter-band structure enables the asymmetric PL emission from the CND suspensions under the blue light illumination.

To further explore the influence of solvent type on the PL emissions, using Eq. (1), the QY value was assessed for various N-functionalized CND suspensions. The variation of QY value for different CND configurations under the blue-light illumination is provided in Fig. 8 (a)‒8(d). The QY values of o-CND suspension in water and ethanol reaches as high as 85 and 99%, respectively (see Fig. 8). The remarkable performance confirms the efficacy of o-CND suspension for industrial applications. The QY value of o-CND suspension decreases to ~2.5% when using PGMEA as the solvent. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 8, the PL emission is quenched for all the CND suspensions in acetone (i.e., the QY values: < 0.9%). This observation further confirms the critical role of the solvent type on the PL emission.

Generally, the QY value can be considered as a crucial index to assess the ratio of the number of photons emitted to the number of photons adsorbed during the radiation-induced process [59]. Thus, the dispersive level of CND suspension, which strongly depends on the relative polarity, has a huge impact on the QY value. As tabulated in Table S1, highly-polar solvents (such as water and ethanol) are favourable for well dispersion of CND suspensions. Since o-CND sample possesses superior hydrophilicity (due to high oxidation level) along with smaller particle size distribution, it emits highly-intense fluorescence, as compared to other two samples. The ultrahigh QY from the o-CND suspension is majorly due to the intense radiative emission mechanism induced by the substitutional N atoms within the electron-withdrawing cycle [33] where the N- and O-rich edge groups are the major contributors for boosting the affinity to high-polarity solvents. Considering the various CND configurations prepared for this study, it is evident that the o-CND sample is a strong emitter within the entire wavelength light source (e.g., white light-emitting-diode device).

For comparison, Table S2 collects the literature concerning functionalized CNDs prepared from PD and different precursors [28–32,60–63]. As observed from Table S2, this study offers an effective hydrothermal method as compared to other methods prepared from o-PD precursor and other isomers. The QY value from the CND suspension in this work can reach as high as 85‒99%. This result demonstrates the importance of optimal parameters setting on the PL efficiency from CNDs even if using the same synthesis method and the identical organic precursor. Accordingly, the unique hydrothermal synthesis method developed in this work provides a straightforward pathway for engineering highly amidized CNDs with high fluorescence efficiency under blue light illumination.

In this work, we have developed a unique hydrothermal technique for growing N-functionalized CNDs using o-, m-, and p-PD isomers. The N/C atomic ratio in the CNDs structure was precisely controlled within the range of 20.2–25.7 at.% through modulating a series of isomer precursors. Employing the hydrothermal method with m-PD precursor results in implanting “lattice N” functionalities, whereas the o-PD precursor facilitates the formation of N-oxide groups on the edges of CNDs. The functionalized CND suspensions in highly polar solvents were found to emit intense fluorescence under the blue light illumination, while the fluorescence from the CND suspensions in PGMEA was quenched. The ultrahigh QYs recorded for the samples (reaching as high as 85‒99% at 450 nm) were majorly due to the effective radiative emission mechanism induced by the substitutional N atoms via high electron-withdrawing possibility and N- and O-rich edge groups serving to enhance the affinity towards high-polarity solvents. The unique hydrothermal synthesis method developed in this work paves the way for designing functionalized CNDs with immediate applications in optical, sensing, energy storage/conversion, and biological devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (Contact No.: MOST 109–2221-E-155–053 and MOST 108–2221-E-155–036-MY3) is greatly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2021.05.017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tailoring fluorescence emissions, quantum yields, and white light emitting from nitrogen-doped graphene and carbon nitride quantum dots
